text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY OUTPATIENT VISIT
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DIVISION OF MEDICAL ONCOLOGY

**VISIT TYPE:** Follow-up — Metastatic Melanoma Surveillance / Shared Decision-Making re: Immunotherapy Discontinuation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 59-year-old woman with Stage IV (M1c) cutaneous melanoma of the back, BRAF V600E-mutant, who presents today for oncology follow-up at approximately 21 months since initiation of checkpoint immunotherapy. She is here to review her most recent surveillance CT imaging and to continue our ongoing discussion about electively stopping nivolumab.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has had a remarkable course. To briefly summarize: she was diagnosed with Stage IV melanoma roughly two years ago when she presented with axillary lymphadenopathy. Staging workup at that time revealed widespread visceral metastatic disease on CT (multiple hepatic lesions, pulmonary nodules, mediastinal adenopathy). Brain MRI was negative. Pathology from left axillary lymph node excision confirmed metastatic melanoma. NGS demonstrated a BRAF V600E mutation with a high tumor mutational burden of 42 mut/Mb.

She was started on dabrafenib plus trametinib (Tafinlar/Mekinist) given the BRAF V600E mutation and burden of disease. She had an initial response confirmed on CT at 8 weeks, and then maintained stable disease at 6 months, but unfortunately progressed at 8 months on BRAF/MEK inhibition. She experienced grade 1 pyrexia and grade 2 hand-foot skin reaction while on dab/tram — both managed without dose interruption.

Given progression on targeted therapy, she was transitioned to ipilimumab plus nivolumab (Yervoy/Opdivo). Repeat brain MRI prior to starting immunotherapy was negative. She received 2 cycles of ipi/nivo before being hospitalized with grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. Ipi was held permanently; she was treated with high-dose steroids and ultimately required infliximab. She recovered well. Post-colitis, she was noted to have grade 1 hypothyroidism (low free T4, mildly elevated TSH) consistent with immune-related thyroiditis, for which she was started on levothyroxine.

She transitioned to nivolumab (Opdivo) maintenance monotherapy following resolution of colitis. Subsequent imaging has shown a progressive and deep response: CT at 5 months post-immunotherapy initiation showed significant partial response with dramatic shrinkage of hepatic and pulmonary disease. CT at 9 months showed near-complete response. CT at 15 months showed complete response by all conventional imaging criteria, confirmed again on today's scan at approximately 21 months — still no evidence of disease on CT.

She has now been in sustained complete radiographic response for well over 6 months. She has received approximately 2 years total of checkpoint immunotherapy (counting the 2 cycles of ipi/nivo induction). We have been discussing over the past several visits whether to continue or discontinue nivolumab.

Today she returns having reviewed additional materials I gave her about the emerging data on treatment discontinuation in patients who achieve CR on anti-PD-1 therapy. She has spoken with her husband and her primary care doctor about this. She feels ready to make a decision today.

She denies any symptoms currently. She feels, by her own report, "essentially like myself again." No fatigue, no diarrhea (notably, her bowel habits have been completely normal since recovering from colitis), no arthralgias, no rash, no vision changes, no dyspnea. She has been very active — back to walking her dog daily, resumed a yoga practice she had abandoned at diagnosis, and returned to full-time teaching this academic year.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: No fevers, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight change. She reports good energy.
Skin: No new lesions of concern to her. No rash. Occasional dryness she attributes to the season.
HEENT: No visual symptoms, no oral lesions.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations.
GI: No diarrhea, no blood in stool, no abdominal pain. Bowel movements 1x daily, formed. No nausea.
GU: No hematuria. Menses ceased prior to diagnosis (menopause at age 54).
Musculoskeletal: Mild intermittent lower back stiffness, which she attributes to prolonged standing at work. No joint swelling.
Neurologic: No new headaches, focal weakness, or sensory changes.
Endocrine: On levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. Reports no cold or heat intolerance, no palpitations. Last TSH was within normal range.
Psychiatric: Mood good. She describes feeling "cautiously optimistic but realistic" — acknowledges some anxiety about stopping treatment.

---

MEDICATIONS:
1. Nivolumab (Opdivo) 480 mg IV q4 weeks — most recent infusion was approximately 4 weeks ago
2. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily
3. Vitamin D3 2000 IU PO daily
4. Occasional ibuprofen PRN (used infrequently; I have counseled her to use acetaminophen preferentially)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

OTHER RELEVANT HISTORY (briefly, as documented at prior visits):
- Dysplastic nevus syndrome, multiple prior excisions of atypical nevi; follows with dermatology
- Hypothyroidism (immune-related, on levothyroxine)
- Prior grade 3 immune-mediated colitis on ipi — resolved; GI cleared for follow-up colonoscopy last year which showed no active colitis
- Hypertension (borderline; not on medications; monitored by PCP)
- Never-smoker
- Social: High school science teacher, married, no children. West African and British descent.
- Family hx: Maternal uncle with cutaneous melanoma

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: BP 122/78, HR 68, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.0°C, Weight stable from last visit.
ECOG Performance Status: 0

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman. In no distress. Appropriately dressed. Pleasant and engaged.
Skin: No worrisome pigmented lesions identified on brief skin survey (neck, face, forearms visible). Multiple well-healed linear scars on back from prior nevus excisions. No rash, no active dermatitis, no palmar-plantar changes.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae clear. PERRL. Oropharynx without lesions.
Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-tender, not enlarged.
Chest / Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, no wheeze.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated. Bowel sounds present.
Lymphadenopathy: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or cervical nodes.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No joint swelling or warmth.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented. CN II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY DATA (from today's labs and recent results):
CBC: WBC 6.1, Hgb 13.6, Plt 214 — all within normal limits
CMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cr 0.82, BUN 14, total bilirubin 0.7, AST 22, ALT 19, alk phos 68 — all normal
TSH: 1.8 uIU/mL (within range on current levothyroxine dose)
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
LFTs have been consistently normal — no late hepatotoxicity.
LDH: 142 U/L (within normal range)

---

IMAGING REVIEWED (CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS, most recent):
Reviewed with patient in office. The previously noted hepatic lesions and pulmonary nodules are no longer visualized. No new lymphadenopathy. No suspicious masses or new lesions identified in any compartment. No pleural effusion. No ascites. Essentially unremarkable study. This is consistent with findings at the 15-month scan — sustained complete response on serial imaging.

Most recent brain MRI (obtained approximately 6 months ago): No intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal disease.

---

ASSESSMENT:
1. Metastatic BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma, Stage IV (M1c): Sustained complete radiographic response now ongoing for at least 6+ months, at 21 months from initiation of checkpoint immunotherapy. She received ipilimumab/nivolumab x2 cycles (limited by grade 3 colitis), followed by nivolumab maintenance for the past ~19 months. Total checkpoint immunotherapy exposure now approximately 2 years.

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism: Stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH and free T4 both in range today. Will continue current dosing.

3. History of grade 3 immune-mediated colitis: Fully resolved. GI has cleared her; colonoscopy last year showed normal mucosa with no evidence of ongoing colitis. Bowel function normal.

---

PLAN:

**RE: Nivolumab discontinuation — Shared Decision-Making:**

Spent considerable time today (approximately 30 minutes of the visit) discussing the question of stopping nivolumab. I reviewed the available data with Ms. Osei-Bonsu:

- Several retrospective series and prospective cohorts have examined outcomes in melanoma patients who discontinue anti-PD-1 therapy after achieving a CR. The data are imperfect but broadly reassuring: the majority of CR patients who stop treatment do not experience relapse, and many who do relapse upon stopping can be rechallenged with checkpoint therapy.
- Her specific situation is favorable: she is BRAF V600E-mutant, which gives her a potential BRAF/MEK re-challenge option if ever needed. She has a high TMB (42 mut/Mb), which is generally associated with durable immunotherapy responses. She achieved a CR, not merely a partial response. She has had a sustained CR now for more than 6 months on imaging.
- Risks of continuing: ongoing rare but cumulative risk of immune-related adverse events (most worrisome given her prior grade 3 colitis — although the risk of a second severe irAE is not zero, she has been tolerating nivo well as monotherapy); cost and inconvenience of monthly infusions; treatment burden.
- Risks of stopping: risk of relapse, though I estimate this is substantially below 50% given the depth and duration of her response; anxiety and psychological adjustment to stopping; uncertainty.

Patient expressed understanding of both sides. She stated that the ongoing burden of monthly infusions has been wearing on her and she is ready to stop, provided I support that decision. She understands she is not stopping due to progression — this is an elective, planned discontinuation after achieving a durable CR. She also asked about re-initiation if there were to be a relapse, and I confirmed we would strongly consider restarting nivo (or ipi/nivo pending her prior colitis history) in that event.

**Decision reached: We will discontinue nivolumab after today's cycle (last infusion received approximately 4 weeks ago — her next infusion would have been today, and she will not receive it).** This marks completion of approximately 2 years total checkpoint immunotherapy exposure.

**Surveillance plan post-discontinuation:**
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 3 months for the first year after stopping
- Brain MRI in approximately 6 months; then annually unless symptomatic or concern arises
- TSH every 6 months; continue levothyroxine
- Dermatology every 6 months (given dysplastic nevus syndrome) — I contacted her dermatologist's office today
- Return to oncology in 3 months with repeat CT (will coordinate timing so she has imaging ready to review at the visit)
- Patient given instructions to call the oncology nurse line immediately with any signs that might suggest relapse (new lymph node, new skin lesion, pain, neurologic symptoms) or immune-related adverse events (diarrhea, rash, shortness of breath)

**Levothyroxine:** Continue 75 mcg daily. PCP to co-manage thyroid going forward; I will send a letter summarizing the immune-related hypothyroidism and recommending annual TFTs at minimum.

**Emotional support:** Patient was visibly emotional at the end of the visit — described it as "bittersweet" to stop treatment that has brought her to this point. I normalized this response and emphasized that this is a success story. She will continue to follow with me for surveillance. No formal referral to psychology at this time but I mentioned the availability of the survivorship clinic and cancer support group.

Time spent in counseling and coordination today: approximately 50 minutes total.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Oncologist, MD]
Division of Medical Oncology
